insights.insight: The protocol produces cryptographically verified purchase confirmations at $0.10 per receipt — compared to $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution. That cost delta is structural, not marginal: the receipt eliminates the attribution problem rather than modeling around it.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the industry's primary cross-site tracking primitives are gone. Verified receipt data — identity-free and cross-ecosystem — fills a gap that first-party data and data brokers cannot, because neither produces signals outside their own walled gardens.

Because no personal identity is attached to the receipt signals, the protocol carries zero compliance overhead under GDPR, CCPA, or state privacy law — no consent flows, no breach liability, no legal review cycles. For brands, that translates directly into procurement speed and reduced legal cost.

Google, Apple, Stripe, and Microsoft each see one slice of the purchase journey — their ecosystem only, identity-attached. DKIM receipt verification cuts across all of them, capturing signals from SaaS subscriptions, app stores, and payment processors in a single queryable dataset no single platform can replicate.

Active purchase signals are now appearing across 13 metro areas plus rural and non-metro geographies — without a referral program or marketing campaign driving it. For brands, that means the dataset is building coverage through actual usage, not incentivized behavior that distorts signal quality.

insights.topic: Technical buyer concentration as data quality indicator
insights.insight: The submitter base skews developer and SaaS-adjacent, reflected in open vendor requests for Notion, Vercel, Render, and DigitalOcean. Technical users submit receipts more consistently and across more vendors, producing a higher-frequency, lower-noise signal than consumer receipt-scanning panels typically deliver.
